images.caption: Figure 1:3D Scene Modeling in Camera Grid.Top: Given reference camera poses, we simulate spatial motion within an empty 3D scene. Orthogonal lines represent the ceiling and floor (red and blue), while vertical lines (yellow) denote the walls.Bottom: Rendering this grid scene from varying viewpoints yields the camera grid, providing a visual representation of camera motion that conditions the model to generate videos with similar trajectories.

Figure 2:Extension of the camera grid to special camera effects.First row:fisheye distortion, where straight lines are rendered as smooth curves via the Kannala–Brandt model.Second row:dolly zoom (Hitchcock zoom), where the subject remains fixed in size while the background undergoes pronounced perspective stretching.

Figure 3:Overview of OmniDirector.Top: OmniDirector represents camera motion via a camera gridGG, which is obtained by rendering the camera poses of a reference videoVVas movement within an empty 3D space.Middle: During training, the camera grid is injected into the MMDiT alongside other control signals via token concatenation.Bottom: At inference, a PE Agent harmoniously integrates various signals into the text prompt, achieving unified multi-signal control.
Figure 5:Qualitative Evaluations.The results demonstrate that OmniDirector accurately clones the camera motion and shot transition semantics of the reference video.

Figure 7:Emergent zero-shot camera control in OmniDirector. During inference, substituting the camera grid with raw RGB videos or Canny edge sequences effectively drives camera motion, demonstrating robust generalization without any retraining.
